1: Paulos kai Timotheos douloi Christou Iesou pasin tois hagiois en Christo Iesou tois ousin en Philippois syn episkopois kai diakonois: Paul and Timothy, douloi Christou Iesou (slaves of Christ Jesus). The self-designation doulos (slave) is Paul's strongest claim of belonging and subordination — the slave has no autonomous will. Episkopois kai diakonois (overseers and deacons) — this is the earliest datable use of these two terms together as distinct offices. Episkopos (overseer) denotes supervisory leadership; diakonos (servant/minister/deacon) denotes functional service. Philippians is uniquely addressed to the leaders alongside the whole congregation.
2: charis hymin kai eirene apo theou patros hemon kai kyriou Iesou Christou: Grace (charis) and peace (eirēnē) — the Pauline greeting fusing Greek (chairein) and Hebrew (shalom) in a theologically loaded form. The dual source (God the Father and Lord Jesus Christ) is Paul's standard binitarian greeting-formula, asserting that the grace and peace flow from both Father and Son as a unified divine source.
3: eucharisto to theo mou epi pase te mneia hymon: 'I thank my God in all my remembrance of you' — the thanksgiving period (1:3-11) is one of Paul's warmest. Eucharistō (I give thanks) is the Greek verb from which eucharist derives. The possessive to theo mou (my God) reflects the personal relationship language of the Psalms.
4: pantote en pase deēsei mou hyper pantōn hymōn meta charas tēn deesin poioumenos: 'always in every prayer of mine for you all, making my prayer with joy' — deēsis (petition, prayer-request) and chara (joy) are the twin notes of the letter's opening. Joy (chara/chairō) appears 16 times in Philippians — the most joy-saturated letter in the Pauline corpus, written from prison.
5: epi tē koinōnia hymōn eis to euangelion apo tēs prōtēs hēmeras achri tou nyn: 'because of your koinōnia in the gospel from the first day until now.' Koinōnia (partnership, participation, fellowship) is the letter's key relational term. It denotes not merely emotional fellowship but active sharing — the Philippians' financial partnership in Paul's missionary work (4:15-17: no church shared with me in giving and receiving except you) and their participation in the gospel project itself. Euangelion (gospel, good news) is the content of the partnership.
6: pepoithōs auto touto hoti ho enarcxamenos en hymin ergon agathon epitelesei achri hēmeras Christou Iesou: 'being confident of this very thing, that he who began a good work in you will bring it to completion at the day of Christ Jesus.' The eschatological confidence is grounded in divine faithfulness: God who initiated (ho enarcxamenos) will also complete (epitelesei). The hēmera Christou Iesou (day of Christ Jesus) — the parousia as the telos of the divine work — appears 3 times in vv.6-11.
7: kathōs estin dikaion emoi touto phronein hyper pantōn hymōn dia to echein me en tē kardia hymas: 'It is right for me to feel this way about you all, because I hold you in my heart.' Phronein (to think, feel, have this mindset) — the key verb of Philippians, appearing 10 times. Kardia (heart) as the seat of commitment and will. Both in my imprisonment and in the defense (apologia) and confirmation (bebaiōsis) of the gospel, you are all partakers (synkoinōnous) with me of grace.
8: martys gar mou ho theos hōs epipothō pantas hymas en splanchnois Christou Iesou: 'For God is my witness, how I yearn for you all with the affection (splanchnois) of Christ Jesus.' Splanchnon (bowels, gut, deep affection) was the ancient term for the seat of compassion — the visceral center of feeling. The genitive Christou Iesou is significant: Paul's yearning is the yearning of Christ himself, not merely Paul's own affection. The apostle has become so identified with Christ that Christ's own compassion flows through him.
9: kai touto proseuchomai hina hē agapē hymōn eti mallon kai mallon perisseuē en epignōsei kai pasē aisthēsei: 'And it is my prayer that your love may abound more and more, with epignōsis (full/genuine knowledge) and all discernment (aisthēsis).' Epignōsis (epi + gnōsis) is the intensified knowledge — thorough, personal, relational knowing rather than mere intellectual acquaintance. Aisthēsis (moral perception, discernment) — the trained capacity to recognize what is genuinely good (v.10: dokimazein ta diapheronta — to approve what is excellent).
10: eis to dokimazein hymas ta diapheronta hina ēte eilikrineis kai aproskopoi eis hēmeral Christou: 'so that you may approve what is excellent (ta diapheronta), and so be pure and blameless for the day of Christ.' Dokimazein (to test, approve through testing) — the word for assaying metal. Ta diapheronta (the things that differ, the excellent things) — Paul prays for the Philippians' moral discernment to identify what genuinely matters among competing goods. Eilikrineis (pure, sincere — possibly from eilē + krinō, judged in sunlight): transparent under examination.
11: peplērōmenoi karpon dikaiosynēs ton dia Iesou Christou eis doxan kai epainon theou: 'filled with the fruit of righteousness that comes through Jesus Christ, to the glory and praise of God.' Karpos dikaiosynēs (fruit of righteousness) echoes the prophetic tradition (Amos 6:12 LXX; Prov 11:30; Isa 32:17: the fruit of righteousness will be peace). The righteousness is not earned by the Philippians but comes through Jesus Christ — its source and channel is christological. Its goal: God's glory and praise.
12: ginōskein de hymas boulomai adelphoi hoti ta kat' eme mallon eis prokopēn tou euangeliou elēlythen: 'I want you to know, brothers, that what has happened to me has really served to advance (prokopēn) the gospel.' Prokopē (progress, advance) was a Stoic philosophical term for moral progress toward the ideal sage. Paul uses it for the gospel's advance — the unexpected instrument being his imprisonment. The paradox: chains became a pulpit.
13: hōste tous desmous mou phaneroys en Christō genesthai en holō tō praitōriō kai tois loipois pasin: 'so that it has become known throughout the whole praetorian guard and to all the rest that my imprisonment is for Christ.' Praitorion (Praetorium) — most naturally the Praetorian Guard headquarters in Rome. En Christō (in/for Christ) — the chains are christologically defined: not punishment but martyrdom. The Praetorian soldiers knew why Paul was imprisoned; the gospel explanation defined the captivity.
14: kai tous pleionas tōn adelphōn en kyriō pepoithotas tois desmois mou perissoteros tolman adeoōs ton logon lalein: 'and most of the brothers, having become confident in the Lord by my imprisonment, are much more bold to speak the word without fear.' The apostle's imprisonment emboldened rather than silenced the broader community. The gospel's advance came through Paul's chains AND through those his chains inspired. Adeoōs (fearlessly, without hesitation) — the courage directly proportional to the apostle's willingness to suffer.
15: tines men kai dia phthonon kai erin, tines de kai di' eudokian ton Christon kēryssousin: 'Some indeed preach Christ from envy and rivalry, but others from good will.' The remarkable acknowledgment: Christ is preached with mixed motives — envy, rivalry, faction. Phthonos (envy) and eris (strife) were standard vices in Greco-Roman catalogues. Paul does not identify these preachers (rival Paulinists? Jewish-Christian missionaries?); the point is their motivation, not their theology.
16: hoi men ex agapēs eidotes hoti eis apologian tou euangeliou keimai: 'the latter do it out of love, knowing that I am put here for the defense of the gospel.' Apologia (defense, legal defense) — the term for a formal judicial defense speech. Paul frames his imprisonment as an ongoing legal defense of the gospel before Roman authorities. Those who preach from love understand this; those who preach from rivalry are, by contrast, presenting Paul's imprisonment as his failure.
17: hoi de ex eritheias ton Christon katangellousin ouch hagnōs, oiomenoi thlipsin egeirein tois desmois mou: 'the former proclaim Christ out of selfish ambition (eritheias), not sincerely, thinking to afflict me in my imprisonment.' Eritheia (selfish ambition, factional self-seeking) — originally the term for day-labor for hire, suggesting mercenary motivation. These preachers apparently believe their success embarrasses the imprisoned Paul or proves his methods deficient.
18: ti gar; plēn hoti panti tropō, eite prophasei eite alētheia, Christos katangelletai, kai en toutō chairō: 'What then? Only that in every way, whether in pretense or in truth, Christ is proclaimed, and in that I rejoice.' The striking pastoral magnanimity: Paul rejoices that Christ is preached even by those whose motives he questions. The object of proclamation — Christ — is what matters, not the purity of the proclaimer's motives. Yes, and I will rejoice — chairō shifts to the future: the joy is not circumstance-dependent.
19: oida gar hoti touto moi apobēsetai eis sōtērian dia tēs hymōn deēseōs kai epichorēgias tou pneumatos Iēsou Christou: 'for I know that through your prayers and the help (epichorēgia) of the Spirit of Jesus Christ this will turn out for my deliverance (sōtērian).' Epichorēgia (supply, provision, full equipping) — originally the wealthy citizen's sponsorship of a chorus. The Spirit's provision is bountiful supply. Sōtēria here likely means both immediate rescue and eschatological salvation — Paul is content with either outcome (vv.20-26).
20: kata tēn apokaradokian kai elpida mou hoti en oudeni aiscynthēsomai alla en pasē parrēsia hōs pantote kai nyn megalynthēsetai Christos en tō sōmati mou: 'as it is my eager expectation (apokaradokia) and hope that I will not be at all ashamed, but that with full courage (parrēsia) now as always Christ will be honored (megalynthēsetai) in my body.' Apokaradokia (eager expectation, neck-craning anticipation) — a Pauline compound (Rom 8:19 also) conveying intense forward-straining. Christ honored/magnified in Paul's body — whether through life or death (v.20b); the body is the apostolic field of Christological display.
21: emoi gar to zēn Christos kai to apothanein kerdos: 'For to me to live is Christ and to die is gain.' The most compressed summary of Paul's existential situation. To zēn Christos: life = Christ, not a christological supplement to normal life but its complete content. To apothanein kerdos: death = gain — not because life is bad but because death means fuller presence with Christ (v.23). The equation is not escapist but participatory: life fully in Christ, death more fully in Christ.
22: ei de to zēn en sarki, touto moi karpos ergou: 'If I am to live in the flesh, that means fruitful labor for me.' The tension between the two goods: continued life produces fruit (karpos ergou) for the congregation; death produces gain for Paul. The apostolic vocation suspends personal preference: the congregation's need takes priority over personal desire (v.24: to remain in the flesh is more necessary for your sake).
23: synechomai de ek tōn dyo, tēn epithymian echōn eis to analyein kai syn Christō einai, pollō [gar] mallon kreisson: 'I am hard pressed between the two. My desire is to depart (analyein) and be with Christ, for that is far better.' Synechomai (to be pressed, held in by, constrained) — Paul is caught between two goods. Analyein (to break camp, weigh anchor, depart) — the departure image evokes a ship leaving harbor. Syn Christō einai (to be with Christ) — the goal of death for believers: personal presence with Christ, not mere dissolution.
24: to de epimenein [en] tē sarki anagkaioteron di' hymas: 'But to remain in the flesh is more necessary on your account.' The apostle's survival calculus is entirely other-directed: not self-preservation or self-interest but the congregation's need. The pastoral logic of cruciform love: the apostle's continued life is a gift given to the congregation, not a personal preference indulged.
25: kai touto pepoithōs oida hoti menō kai paramenō pasin hymin eis tēn hymōn prokopēn kai charan tēs pisteōs: 'Convinced of this, I know that I will remain and continue with you all, for your progress (prokopēn) and joy in the faith.' Prokopē again — the Philippians' advance in faith and joy is the purpose of Paul's continued ministry. The joy (chara tēs pisteōs — joy of faith) encompasses both the apostle's joy and the congregation's joy in the faith they share.
26: hina to kauchēma hymōn perisseuē en Christō Iēsou en emoi dia tēs emēs parousias palin pros hymas: 'so that in me you may have ample cause to glory in Christ Jesus, because of my coming (parousias) to you again.' Kauchēma (boast, ground of boasting) — in Christ Jesus: the boasting is not in Paul himself but in Christ through Paul. Parousia (presence, coming) — the same word used for Christ's eschatological coming; Paul's anticipated visit is a mini-parousia of the apostle to his congregation.
27: monon axiōs tou euangeliou tou Christou politeuesthe: 'Only let your manner of life (politeuesthe) be worthy of the gospel of Christ.' Politeuesthai (to live as a citizen, to conduct oneself as a member of a polity) — the civic-political verb from polis (city). The Philippians as Roman colonists understood citizenship obligations; Paul redirects their civic identity: the gospel defines the polity in which they live. This verse is the hinge from personal matters (vv.12-26) to community exhortation (1:27-4:9).
28: kai mē ptyromenoi en mēdeni hypo tōn antikeimenōn: 'and not frightened in anything by your opponents.' Ptyromenoi (frightened, startled — used of horses shying) — the congregation's steadfastness is itself a sign (endeixis): to the opponents it signals destruction; to the Philippians it signals their salvation, and this from God. The courage of the congregation in the face of opposition is itself apologetic witness.
29: hoti hymin echaristhē to hyper Christou, ou monon to eis auton pisteuein alla kai to hyper autou paschein: 'For it has been granted (echaristhē — a grace-gift) to you that for the sake of Christ you should not only believe in him but also suffer for his sake.' The remarkable claim: suffering for Christ is a charisma (grace-gift), not a punishment or failure. The suffering and the faith are equally granted by divine grace — both are God's gifts to the congregation.
30: ton auton agōna echontes hoion eidete en emoi kai nyn akouete en emoi: 'engaged in the same conflict (agōna) that you saw I had and now hear that I still have.' Agōn (contest, struggle — from the athletic and gladiatorial arena) — the shared struggle of apostle and congregation. The Philippians saw Paul's first conflict when he was imprisoned in Philippi (Acts 16:19-24); now they hear of his Roman imprisonment. The apostolic pattern of suffering is the congregation's own pattern.
